meta.notes: Novel AI-generated PSBB practice questions for the SpeedyCAT proof-of-concept. NOT real MCAT/AAMC content. Text-only. Chunk 9 of 10.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of ethnocentrism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Feeling poor despite a solid income because one's neighbors are far wealthier.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sweeping public alarm that a new video game is corrupting the nation's youth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immigrant families adopting the dominant language and customs over generations until differences fade.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assuming that eating with one's hands instead of utensils is simply backward and wrong.

questions.explanation: An example of ethnocentrism is: assuming that eating with one's hands instead of utensils is simply backward and wrong. In general, ethnocentrism refers to the tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the spread of cultural traits, ideas, or objects from one group or society to another?

choices.text: relative deprivation

choices.label: B
choices.text: a ritual

choices.label: C
choices.text: cultural diffusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: values

questions.explanation: This description matches cultural diffusion, which is the spread of cultural traits, ideas, or objects from one group or society to another. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes a subculture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The rewards and punishments a group uses to enforce conformity to its norms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The idea that deviance results from the labels society attaches to certain acts and people.

questions.explanation: A subculture is best described as a group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of sanctions?

questions.explanation: An example of sanctions is: a fine issued for littering alongside public praise given for a generous act. In general, sanctions refers to the rewards and punishments a group uses to enforce conformity to its norms. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes differential association?

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of resource mobilization theory?

questions.explanation: An example of resource mobilization theory is: a movement gaining momentum only after securing funding, volunteers, and press coverage. questions.stem: A research team concludes that a new therapy reduces anxiety, but in reality the therapy has no effect. Which error have they made?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A sampling error that always makes results invalid.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No error, because any positive finding must be correct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A Type I error, that is, a false positive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A Type II error, that is, a false negative.

questions.explanation: Concluding that an effect exists when it truly does not is a false positive, which is a Type I error.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the bystander effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: People being less likely to help a victim when other people are present.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A generalized and often oversimplified belief about a group of people.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gaining compliance with a large request by first securing a small one.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Piaget's stage in which infants know the world through their senses and actions.

questions.explanation: The bystander effect is best described as people being less likely to help a victim when other people are present. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a stereotype?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assuming that all members of a profession share the same personality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A teenager trying out different roles and values to arrive at a personal identity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An early window in which exposure to language is essential for fluent acquisition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Following an authority's order to deliver what one believes are harmful shocks.

questions.explanation: An example of a stereotype is: assuming that all members of a profession share the same personality. In general, a stereotype refers to a generalized and often oversimplified belief about a group of people. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: attitude change driven by careful evaluation of strong arguments?

choices.text: emotion-focused coping

choices.label: D

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the Yerkes-Dodson law?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coping by managing one's emotional response to a stressor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Engaging in an activity to earn a reward or avoid a punishment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive stress that feels motivating and manageable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The principle that performance peaks at a moderate level of arousal.

questions.explanation: The Yerkes-Dodson law is best described as the principle that performance peaks at a moderate level of arousal. The other options describe different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of extrinsic motivation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Friends offering encouragement and practical help during a difficult time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Becoming worn down and prone to illness after prolonged, unrelenting stress.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Feeling calm or afraid based first on judging whether a situation is threatening.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Studying only to win a prize rather than out of interest.

questions.explanation: An example of extrinsic motivation is: studying only to win a prize rather than out of interest. In general, extrinsic motivation refers to engaging in an activity to earn a reward or avoid a punishment. The other options illustrate different concepts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: homeostasis

choices.label: D

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes leptin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Passive resignation that follows repeated exposure to uncontrollable events.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The set of internal and external factors that energize and direct behavior.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hormone released by fat cells that signals satiety to the brain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The comfort and resources provided by one's social network during stress.

questions.explanation: Leptin is best described as a hormone released by fat cells that signals satiety to the brain. The other options describe different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the Schachter-Singer theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Seeking out a thrilling activity when life feels boring and understimulating.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reading a racing heart as excitement or as fear depending on the situation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The chain of glands that releases stress hormones during a prolonged threat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A hormone that rises with fat stores and reduces appetite.

questions.explanation: An example of the Schachter-Singer theory is: reading a racing heart as excitement or as fear depending on the situation. In general, the Schachter-Singer theory refers to the theory that emotion depends on arousal plus a cognitive label for it. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes secondary appraisal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The three-stage pattern of the body's response to prolonged stress.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A trait involving the pursuit of varied, novel, and intense experiences.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The evaluation of one's resources and options for coping with a stressor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The theory that a cognitive appraisal of a situation precedes and shapes emotion.

questions.explanation: Secondary appraisal is best described as the evaluation of one's resources and options for coping with a stressor. The other options describe different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the fight-or-flight response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The draining pressure of demands that feel beyond one's control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A worker staying engaged when given choice, skill-building, and connection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A pounding heart and tensed muscles readying a person to run from danger.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Feeling fear and a pounding heart at the very same instant when facing danger.

questions.explanation: An example of the fight-or-flight response is: a pounding heart and tensed muscles readying a person to run from danger. In general, the fight-or-flight response refers to the body's rapid physiological mobilization to confront or flee a threat. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes emotion-focused coping?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The final stress stage, in which the body's resources become depleted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coping by managing one's emotional response to a stressor.

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of burnout?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The draining pressure of demands that feel beyond one's control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: First judging whether an unexpected loud noise is dangerous.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The long-term bodily toll of years of unrelieved stress.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A nurse feeling drained, cynical, and ineffective after months of overwork.

questions.explanation: An example of burnout is: a nurse feeling drained, cynical, and ineffective after months of overwork. In general, burnout refers to physical and emotional exhaustion resulting from prolonged occupational stress. The other options illustrate different concepts.
